title: Ross River and Barmah Forest Virus

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Ross River virus (RRV) and Barmah Forest virus (BFV) are mosquito-borne alphaviruses endemic to Australia and among the most common arboviral infections in regional WA. Both viruses cause a similar syndrome of polyarthritis, rash, and fatigue.

**MANDATORY NOTIFIABLE DISEASES in Western Australia**

**Transmission**
- Transmitted by *Aedes* and *Culex* mosquitoes
- Peak risk: dusk and dawn near water bodies (wetlands, rivers, dams, flooded areas)
- **Not transmitted person to person**
- Incubation: 3–21 days (typically 7–10 days)

**Mine Site Relevance**
- Extremely common cause of musculoskeletal illness in regional WA workers
- Frequently confused with septic arthritis, other infections, or work-related injuries
- Significant cause of fitness-for-work impairment - arthralgia can persist for months
- Mine sites near wetlands, irrigation areas, or flood plains carry highest risk

**Seasonal Pattern**
- Year-round in tropical WA; spring–summer peak in southern WA
- Risk increases significantly after flooding events

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Ross River Virus - Classic Presentation**
**Arthropathy (hallmark):**
- Symmetrical polyarthralgia/polyarthritis - most prominent in wrists, knees, ankles, small joints of hands and feet
- Morning stiffness lasting >30 minutes
- Joint swelling (less common than pain)
- Migratory - moves from joint to joint
- Can be severely debilitating - unable to walk, grip, or perform physical work

**Rash:**
- Maculopapular rash on trunk and limbs (present in 50%)
- May be pruritic
- Resolves over 1–2 weeks

**Constitutional:**
- Fatigue (often profound and disproportionate to other symptoms)
- Low-grade fever - often absent
- Myalgia
- Headache

**Timing:** arthralgia typically develops with or after rash; may outlast rash by months

**Barmah Forest Virus**
- Clinically similar to RRV
- Rash often more prominent and pruritic
- Arthralgia typically less severe than RRV
- Fatigue and malaise prominent

**Course**
- Most patients recover within 3–6 months
- 10–30% develop prolonged symptoms >6 months
- No deaths directly attributable in immunocompetent individuals

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**Diagnosis Requires Serology**
- Clinical diagnosis alone is unreliable - many conditions mimic RRV/BFV
- Serology (IgM and IgG for RRV and BFV) is required for definitive diagnosis
- IgM becomes detectable within 7–10 days of symptom onset
- Collect pathology and arrange review at nearest medical facility

**Differential Diagnosis**
| Condition | Key Differentiating Features |
|---|---|
| Septic arthritis | Usually monoarticular, hot swollen joint, fever, systemically unwell - **urgent** |
| Reactive arthritis | History of preceding GI or GU infection |
| Rheumatoid arthritis | Prolonged, systemic features, older age group |
| Gout | Usually monoarticular, severe, red/hot, uric acid history |
| Viral arthritis (other) | Recent viral illness, serology |

**Septic Arthritis Must Be Excluded**
- A single hot, swollen, extremely painful joint in an unwell patient requires joint aspiration to exclude septic arthritis - see medical team urgently

**No Specific Treatment - Symptomatic Only**
- No antiviral treatment exists
- NSAIDs and rest are the mainstay

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
**Immediate Assessment**
- Vital signs: temperature, heart rate, blood pressure
- Assess for septic arthritis (monoarticular hot joint, fever, systemically unwell) - if present, urgent referral
- Document all affected joints, rash distribution

**Symptomatic Treatment**
**Analgesia:**
- Ibuprofen 400 mg TDS with food (first-line - anti-inflammatory effect)
- Paracetamol 1 g QID for breakthrough pain or if NSAIDs contraindicated
- Avoid opioids (habituation risk with prolonged course)

**Rest:**
- Rest affected joints during acute phase
- Graduated return to activity as symptoms allow - avoid overexertion which worsens symptoms

**Fitness for Work**
- Workers with RRV/BFV are **not able to perform physical duties** while acutely symptomatic
- Significant grip weakness, reduced mobility, and profound fatigue impair safe performance of physical tasks
- Document and issue modified duties or off-work certification as appropriate

**Notification**
- Report to WA Health
- Request serology: "RRV and BFV IgM/IgG" through nearest laboratory

**Referral**
- All suspected cases should be referred to a GP or medical officer for serology and formal diagnosis
- Evacuation generally not required unless complication suspected

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
**Prolonged Fatigue and Joint Pain**
- Explain to patient: symptoms can persist for weeks to months - this is expected
- Reassurance is therapeutic
- Exercise: gentle, graduated activity (hydrotherapy, swimming) preferred to bed rest
- Avoid high-impact activity until symptoms improve

**Management of Severely Debilitating Arthropathy**
- Short course oral prednisolone (in consultation with doctor) may help in severe cases
- Hydroxychloroquine has been used for prolonged arthropathy - specialist referral
- Physiotherapy consultation for joint protection, exercise prescription

**Complication Recognition**
- Neurological complications (encephalitis, myelitis) - rare but documented; any neurological symptoms warrant urgent review
- Myocarditis - extremely rare; chest pain or arrhythmia in an RRV patient requires ECG and urgent assessment

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Worker Education**
- No vaccine available for RRV or BFV
- Mosquito bite prevention is the only protection: DEET repellent, long sleeves/trousers at dawn/dusk, screen tents, avoid breeding habitats
- Workers should report new-onset joint pain to the medical team - early diagnosis avoids unnecessary investigations

**Return to Work**
- Physical duties: return when pain allows safe task performance - no fixed timeline
- Sedentary/administrative duties: can usually continue
- Some workers are unable to return to heavy physical work for 3–6 months
- Document clearly that incapacity is related to a notifiable arboviral infection
